---
date: "2026-06-22"
---

## The Shared Fix: Clean Controls and Transparent Aggregation

- Part 10 — clean controls, transparent aggregation, and spec discipline

::: notes
Welcome back to Chapter four-point-two, part ten of twelve. In part nine, de Chaisemartin and D'Haultfœuille generalized the crisis to negative cell weights — the same disease Goodman-Bacon found in cohort pairs. Now we step back from the wreckage and state the shared cure that every modern estimator implements, the two phrases that carry it, and the spec discipline it demands.
:::

## The Shared Fix

- Root cause: OLS *picked* the comparisons, blindly
- **Never** let an already-treated unit serve as a control
- Compare each cohort only to **never-** or **not-yet-treated** units
- Estimate clean group-time effects one at a time
- Aggregate with weights you choose and can write down

::: notes
Step back and ask what went wrong at the root. The pooled regression failed because it let OLS pick the comparisons — and OLS, blind to which units are clean controls, included the forbidden ones with whatever weights the timing-variance arithmetic dictated. Every modern heterogeneity-robust estimator fixes this by taking the comparison-selection and the weighting out of OLS's hands and doing both deliberately. They differ in bookkeeping, but share one idea, and if you remember nothing else, remember this. Never let an already-treated unit serve as a control. For each newly-treated cohort, estimate its effect by comparing it only to clean controls — units that are never treated, or not yet treated. Estimate these clean group-time effects one at a time, then aggregate them into whatever summary you want, using weights you choose and can write down.
:::

## Two Phrases Carry the Load

- **Clean control:** untreated *at the moment* used as counterfactual
- Never-treated (clean forever) or not-yet-treated (clean until adoption)
- **Transparent aggregation:** estimate a group-time grid, then average
- By event time, calendar time, or one overall ATT
- An audit trail replaces the opaque single $\hat\beta$

::: notes
Two phrases carry the whole load. Clean control means a unit that is untreated at the moment it is being used as a counterfactual — either never-treated, clean forever, or not-yet-treated, clean until its own adoption date, usable as a control only in the window before it switches on. Transparent aggregation means that instead of one regression spitting out one opaque coefficient, you first estimate a whole panel of group-time effects, then explicitly average them — by event time for a dynamics plot, by calendar time, or into a single overall number with sensible non-negative weights, typically each cohort weighted by its size. The opacity that hid the crisis for thirty years is replaced by an audit trail.
:::

## Spec Discipline for Staggered DiD

- Name the **control group**: never-treated, not-yet-treated, or both
- Never-treated may differ systematically; not-yet-treated shrinks over time
- Name the **aggregation weights** — event-, calendar-, or overall-time
- Parallel trends now stated **per cohort**
- Three estimators implement this one paragraph

::: notes
The spec discipline gains two lines for staggered designs. First, name the control group explicitly: are your clean comparisons against never-treated units, not-yet-treated units, or both? This matters because never-treated units may differ systematically from adopters — a state that never regulates insurers is a different animal — whereas not-yet-treated units are eventual adopters and often a more credible counterfactual, but using them shrinks your control pool as more cohorts switch on. Second, name the aggregation weights: an event-time average, a calendar-time average, and a single overall effect can be genuinely different numbers, and the reader must know which one your headline reports. The identifying assumption is still parallel trends, but now stated per cohort: each adopting cohort's untreated potential outcome would have moved parallel to its clean control group's. The three estimators we meet next — Callaway and Sant'Anna, Sun and Abraham, and Borusyak, Jaravel, and Spiess — are three implementations of this one paragraph.
:::

## Key Takeaway

::: callout-tip
## Key takeaway
The cure is one paragraph: **never use an already-treated control**; compare each cohort only to never- or not-yet-treated units; estimate a group-time grid; aggregate with weights you write down. Name your **control group** and your **aggregation weights** explicitly, and state parallel trends **per cohort**. Three estimators implement exactly this.
:::

::: notes
To summarize part ten: the root failure was letting OLS pick the comparisons blindly. The cure takes that choice back. Never use an already-treated unit as a control; for each newly-treated cohort, compare only to never-treated or not-yet-treated units; estimate clean group-time effects one at a time; and aggregate them with weights you choose and can defend. Two phrases carry it — clean control and transparent aggregation — and they replace the opaque single coefficient with an audit trail. The spec discipline gains two lines: name your control group and your aggregation weights, and state parallel trends per cohort. In part eleven, we meet the first and cleanest implementation: Callaway and Sant'Anna's group-time average treatment effects.
:::
